title: Evergrande's Collapse: China's $60 Trillion Property Reckoning

contentBlocks.text: In January 2024, a Hong Kong court ordered the liquidation of China Evergrande Group, once the world's most valuable real estate developer, now carrying $340 billion in liabilities—the largest corporate default in history. Evergrande's collapse isn't an isolated failure; it's the visible edge of a systemic crisis in China's property sector, which represents roughly 25-30% of Chinese GDP when including construction, materials, and related services. Across China, 20 million apartments sit unfinished and pre-sold—families paid developers in full for homes that may never be completed. The total value of China's residential real estate is estimated at $60 trillion, twice the size of China's entire economy, and property prices in major cities have fallen 20-30% from 2021 peaks. This isn't a market correction—it's the unwinding of a multi-decade growth model where property development was the primary engine of GDP growth, local government revenue, and household wealth storage. China is attempting to engineer a controlled deleveraging of the world's largest asset bubble without triggering financial crisis or social unrest. The stakes aren't just economic—they're political survival for the CCP.

China's property model was elegantly designed for growth but catastrophic for stability. Local governments, prohibited from raising taxes or issuing bonds directly, financed development by selling land-use rights to developers. Developers borrowed heavily to buy land, built apartments, and sold them pre-construction to households who paid in full upfront. This generated immediate cash for developers, revenue for governments, and GDP growth from construction. Banks lent freely because property was the collateral, and prices always rose. The result: a self-reinforcing boom where rising prices justified borrowing, and borrowing funded construction that drove growth. Households, with few investment alternatives due to capital controls, parked savings in property, driving prices higher. By 2020, property and related sectors accounted for 25-30% of GDP—far higher than any major economy.

The 'Three Red Lines' policy in 2020 was Beijing's attempt to deflate the bubble gradually. The rules limited developers' liability-to-asset ratios, net gearing, and cash-to-short-term debt ratios—essentially forcing deleveraging. Evergrande, with debt exceeding 200% of equity, violated all three lines. The policy was intended to prevent overleveraged developers from getting more debt. But it triggered the crisis it meant to prevent: developers couldn't refinance maturing debt, projects halted, and households stopped buying apartments, fearing incompletion. Sales collapsed, developers defaulted, and property prices fell. Beijing learned what Japan discovered in 1991: you can't gently deflate a bubble once expectations shift. Once households believe prices will fall, they stop buying, confirming the decline.

The 20 million unfinished apartments represent a political crisis, not just economic. These aren't speculative investors—they're middle-class families who paid life savings for homes that developers can't complete because cash flow dried up. In 2022, homebuyers in over 100 cities organized mortgage payment strikes, refusing to pay for apartments that might never exist. The strikes forced Beijing to act: local governments established 'guarantee funds' to complete projects, and banks extended credit to distressed developers. But the scale is overwhelming—estimates suggest $500 billion is needed to finish stalled projects. This is a direct wealth transfer from the financial system to households to prevent social instability. Property isn't just investment in China; it's the social contract—work hard, save, buy property, achieve middle-class security. That contract is breaking.

Local government finances are the transmission mechanism for broader crisis. Provincial and municipal governments derive 30-40% of revenue from land sales. As property demand collapsed, land sales fell 50% in 2022-2023. This gutted local budgets, forcing cuts to infrastructure, public services, and social programs—exactly when stimulus was needed. Local Government Financing Vehicles (LGFVs), the entities that borrow against land to fund development, hold an estimated $9 trillion in debt. If property prices keep falling, the collateral securing that debt diminishes, risking defaults that spread to the banking system. Beijing faces a dilemma: stimulus requires local government spending, but local governments are broke because property collapsed. The central government must either bail out provinces (expensive, politically difficult) or accept economic stagnation.

The rebalancing imperative is clear but painful. China's economy must shift from property-driven growth to consumption, innovation, and services. But this transition requires accepting lower GDP growth, reallocating capital from construction to productive sectors, and allowing household wealth (concentrated in property) to decline—politically toxic when the CCP's legitimacy rests on delivering prosperity. Xi Jinping's 'Common Prosperity' agenda aims to reduce inequality and shift growth models, but property deleveraging accelerates inequality (wealthy own multiple properties, poor own leveraged single homes declining in value). The economic logic is clear; the political path is not. Japan's Lost Decades show that unwinding a property bubble can take generations—and Japan is a democracy where governments can be voted out. China must do it under one-party rule where economic failure threatens political stability.

Evergrande's liquidation marks the transition from controlled deleveraging to managed crisis. The property sector, which drove China's growth for three decades, is contracting—prices falling, developers defaulting, local governments broke, and household wealth eroding. Beijing is providing targeted support to finish stalled projects and prevent financial contagion, but it's refusing the comprehensive bailout that would reflate the bubble. This is a deliberate policy choice: accept short-term pain to avoid long-term catastrophe. The stakes are immense—$60 trillion in residential property, 70% of household wealth, 25% of GDP, and the legitimacy of one-party rule. The global implications are equally significant: China has been the marginal source of global growth for two decades, and property was the engine of that growth. A prolonged property recession means structurally lower Chinese growth, reduced demand for commodities (iron ore, copper, coal), and ripple effects across emerging markets that supply China. The optimistic scenario is a Japan-style slow deflation—painful but manageable, with gradual rebalancing toward consumption. The pessimistic scenario is disorderly deleveraging that triggers banking crisis, local government defaults, and social instability that the CCP suppresses through political control rather than economic adjustment. Either way, the era of China's property-fueled hypergrowth is over, and the world is only beginning to price in what that means for everything from commodity demand to geopolitical assertiveness. Evergrande isn't the problem—it's the symptom of a growth model that worked brilliantly until it didn't, and now no one knows how to unwind it without breaking something fundamental.

quiz.question: How did China's property development model generate growth, and why was it structurally fragile?
options: The government directly built all housing using tax revenue
Local governments sold land to developers who borrowed heavily; buyers paid upfront for pre-construction homes, creating cash flow that drove GDP growth—but it depended on perpetually rising prices
All property was owned by the central government and rented to citizens
Developers were prohibited from borrowing and had to use only equity financing
quiz.explanation: The model was self-reinforcing but fragile: local governments sold land-use rights for revenue; developers borrowed against land to build; households paid upfront for unfinished apartments; cash funded construction that drove GDP. This worked as long as prices rose, justifying borrowing and sales. Once expectations shifted and prices fell, households stopped buying, developers couldn't complete projects, and local revenue collapsed—the whole mechanism reversed.

quiz.question: Why did the 20 million unfinished pre-sold apartments create a political crisis for Beijing?
options: The apartments were all owned by foreign investors who threatened sanctions
Middle-class families paid life savings for homes that may never be completed, breaking the social contract that hard work and saving lead to security—mortgage strikes forced government intervention
The unfinished apartments violated international building codes
Developers had built apartments that no one wanted to buy
quiz.explanation: This wasn't just financial loss—it was betrayal of the middle-class social contract. Families who saved and paid in full for apartments found projects halted. Mortgage payment strikes in 100+ cities demonstrated this was a political crisis: citizens who followed the rules felt cheated. Beijing's response—bailout funds to complete projects—shows property isn't just economic policy, it's the foundation of CCP legitimacy through prosperity.

quiz.question: What dilemma does the property crisis create for local government finances and economic stimulus?
options: Local governments have too much revenue and don't know how to spend it

Land sales provided 30-40% of local revenue; property collapse eliminated this, leaving governments broke exactly when stimulus spending is needed—forcing central government bailouts or accepting stagnation
Local governments are prohibited from all forms of borrowing
Property taxes have increased to replace land sale revenue
quiz.explanation: Local governments financed 30-40% of budgets through land sales to developers. Property collapse meant sales fell 50%, gutting local budgets when stimulus was most needed. Local Government Financing Vehicles hold $9 trillion in debt collateralized by land whose value is falling. Beijing faces a choice: bail out provinces (expensive), or accept that the government entities that typically execute stimulus are broke and can't respond.
